questions.q: Describe the structure and function of the following parts of the floral organ: sepals, petals, stamens, and carpels. Explain their role in pollination and seed production.
questions.answer: The floral organ consists of four main parts: sepals, petals, stamens, and carpels. Sepals are green, leaf-like structures that protect the delicate flower bud. They are usually green and often resemble leaves. Petals, on the other hand, are colourful structures that attract pollinators such as bees, butterflies, and hummingbirds. They are often fragrant and provide nectar to these pollinators. Stamens are the male reproductive organs of the flower, consisting of a filament and an anther. The anther produces pollen, which contains the male gametes. Carpels are the female reproductive organs of the flower, consisting of a stigma, style, and ovary. The stigma receives the pollen grains, while the ovary contains the ovules where fertilization takes place. The role of these parts in pollination is crucial, as they attract pollinators and facilitate the transfer of pollen. In seed production, the carpels produce ovules that are fertilized by the pollen grains, resulting in the development of seeds.
marking_scheme: Describe the structure and function of sepals (1 mark)
Describe the structure and function of petals (1 mark)
Describe the structure and function of stamens (1 mark)
Describe the structure and function of carpels (1 mark)
Explain the role of these parts in pollination and seed production (1 mark)

questions.q: Explain the process of double fertilization in angiosperms. Describe the events that occur during this process and their significance.
questions.answer: Double fertilization is a unique process that occurs in angiosperms, where two male gametes fertilize two different cells in the ovary. The first fertilization event occurs when a pollen grain germinates and produces a pollen tube that grows down the style and into the ovary. The pollen tube delivers two male gametes: one fertilizes the egg cell to form a zygote, while the other fertilizes the central cell to form a triploid primary endosperm nucleus. This process is significant because it ensures the formation of a diploid zygote and a triploid endosperm. The zygote develops into a seedling, while the endosperm provides nutrients to the developing embryo.
marking_scheme: Describe the process of double fertilization (2 marks)
Explain the events that occur during double fertilization (2 marks)
Describe the significance of double fertilization (1 mark)

questions.q: Describe the types of fruits and their characteristics. Explain the role of fruits in the reproduction and dispersal of angiosperms.
questions.answer: Fruits are the mature ovary of a plant, containing seeds. There are three main types of fruits: fleshy fruits, dry fruits, and accessory fruits. Fleshy fruits, such as apples and berries, are characterized by a soft, edible pericarp that surrounds the seeds. Dry fruits, such as beans and grains, are characterized by a hard, indehiscent pericarp that does not open to release the seeds. Accessory fruits, such as strawberries and figs, have a fleshy part that is not derived from the ovary itself. Fruits play a crucial role in the reproduction and dispersal of angiosperms. They attract animals to eat the fruit and disperse the seeds, often in a new location. This process is known as zoochory.
marking_scheme: Describe the types of fruits (2 marks)
Explain the characteristics of fleshy fruits (1 mark)
Explain the characteristics of dry fruits (1 mark)
Explain the role of fruits in the reproduction and dispersal of angiosperms (1 mark)

questions.q: Explain the significance of the taproot and adventitious root systems in plants. Describe the types of taproots and their characteristics.
questions.answer: The taproot and adventitious root systems are two types of root systems found in plants. Taproots are characterized by a single, large root that grows straight down into the soil, while adventitious roots are formed from stems or leaves and grow in various directions. Taproots are significant because they allow plants to absorb water and nutrients from deep in the soil, while adventitious roots are important for plants that grow in shallow soil or on rocky surfaces. There are several types of taproots, including the conical taproot, which is found in carrots and beets, and the fleshy taproot, which is found in sweet potatoes and turnips.
marking_scheme: Explain the significance of the taproot system (2 marks)
Describe the significance of the adventitious root system (2 marks)
Describe the types of taproots and their characteristics (1 mark)

questions.q: Describe the structure and function of the phloem tissue in plants. Explain the role of phloem in the transport of sugars and other organic compounds.
questions.answer: Phloem is a type of vascular tissue found in plants that is responsible for the transport of sugars, amino acids, and other organic compounds produced by photosynthesis. It consists of sieve cells, companion cells, and phloem parenchyma cells. Sieve cells have pores that allow the free movement of organic compounds, while companion cells are responsible for loading these compounds into the sieve cells. Phloem parenchyma cells store and transport these compounds. The role of phloem in the transport of sugars and other organic compounds is crucial for plant growth and development. It allows plants to distribute these compounds to various parts of the plant, including roots, stems, and leaves.
marking_scheme: Describe the structure of phloem tissue (2 marks)
Explain the function of phloem tissue (2 marks)
Explain the role of phloem in the transport of sugars and other organic compounds (1 mark)
